---
title: "Ch 9 — Straight Lines"
subtitle: "Slope, intercepts, distance, angle: every fact about every line in the plane"
lesson-id: "cbse11.straight-lines"
tier: "cbse11"
ncert-class: 11
ncert-chapter: 9
ncert-chapter-title: "Straight Lines"
board-exam: false
prerequisites: ["cbse10.coordinate-geometry", "cbse9.linear-equations-two-vars"]
unlocks: ["cbse11.conic-sections-circle-parabola", "cbse12.linear-programming"]
applied-domains: ["game-dev", "ai-ml", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 45
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest before you begin.

1. Can you plot a point given its $(x, y)$ coordinates?
2. Do you know what *slope* means, in terms of rise over run?
3. Can you use the distance formula $\sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$?
4. Have you ever written a line as $y = mx + c$?

Three confident yeses — read on.
:::

## Touch

Take graph paper. Mark a point at $(2, 3)$ and another at $(6, 5)$. Draw
a straight line through them. The line goes "up" as you move "right".
For every $4$ units right, the line rises $2$ units — the slope is
$2/4 = 1/2$.

The widget below lets you build a line by two methods: by slope and a
point, or by two points.

```{ojs}
viewof method = Inputs.select(["point + slope", "two points"], {label: "Method"})
```
```{ojs}
viewof slope_m = Inputs.range([-3, 3], {value: 0.5, step: 0.1, label: "slope m (if used)"})
```
```{ojs}
viewof p1x = Inputs.range([-5, 5], {value: 2, step: 0.1, label: "x₁"})
```
```{ojs}
viewof p1y = Inputs.range([-5, 5], {value: 3, step: 0.1, label: "y₁"})
```
```{ojs}
viewof p2x = Inputs.range([-5, 5], {value: 6, step: 0.1, label: "x₂"})
```
```{ojs}
viewof p2y = Inputs.range([-5, 5], {value: 5, step: 0.1, label: "y₂"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  let m, c, eq;
  if (method === "point + slope") {
    m = slope_m;
    c = p1y - m * p1x;
    eq = `y = ${m.toFixed(2)} x + ${c.toFixed(2)}`;
  } else {
    if (Math.abs(p2x - p1x) < 0.001) {
      eq = `x = ${p1x.toFixed(2)} (vertical, slope undefined)`;
      m = "undefined";
    } else {
      m = (p2y - p1y) / (p2x - p1x);
      c = p1y - m * p1x;
      eq = `y = ${m.toFixed(2)} x + ${c.toFixed(2)} (slope ${m.toFixed(2)})`;
    }
  }
  return html`<div style="font-family:system-ui;line-height:1.7;">
    <div><b>Equation of line:</b> ${eq}</div>
    <div style="margin-top:0.4em;color:#666;">
      Two facts pin a line: a point + a direction (slope), or two points.
    </div>
  </div>`;
}
```

Move the sliders. Two facts — a point and a direction, or two points —
pin every line uniquely.

Try two extreme cases by hand. Set both points so $y_1 = y_2$: the line
goes flat and the slope reads $0$. Now set $x_1 = x_2$ instead: the
widget refuses to give a number — the slope is *undefined*, not zero,
not infinity. Those are the two special cases that the slope formula
$m = (y_2 - y_1)/(x_2 - x_1)$ politely warns you about every time the
denominator threatens to vanish.

## Wonder

Sit with these.

1. What is the slope of a perfectly *horizontal* line? A perfectly
   *vertical* line?
2. Two lines have slopes $2$ and $-1/2$. What is the angle between
   them? Why is the answer cleaner than for $2$ and $5$?
3. The line $y = 3x + 1$ and the line $3x - y + 1 = 0$ — same line, or
   different?
4. From an outside point $P$, what is the *shortest distance* to a
   line $\ell$? In which direction must you walk?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice

(2) is the seed of the **perpendicularity criterion**: $m_1 m_2 = -1$.
(4) is the **distance from a point to a line** — a one-line formula.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Slope from two points

Find the slope of the line through $(1, 2)$ and $(5, 14)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$m = \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$m = \dfrac{14 - 2}{5 - 1} = \dfrac{12}{4} = 3$.
</details>

### Problem 2 — Equation from point and slope

Find the equation of the line with slope $-2$ passing through $(3, 1)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Point-slope form: $y - y_1 = m(x - x_1)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$y - 1 = -2(x - 3) \Rightarrow y = -2x + 7$, or $2x + y - 7 = 0$.
</details>

### Problem 3 — Equation from two points

Find the equation of the line through $(1, 1)$ and $(4, 7)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$m = (7-1)/(4-1) = 2$. Then $y - 1 = 2(x - 1) \Rightarrow y = 2x - 1$.
</details>

### Problem 4 — Parallel and perpendicular

Two lines have slopes $3$ and $m_2$. State the condition for parallel,
and the condition for perpendicular.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Parallel:** $m_2 = 3$ (same slope, different intercept).

**Perpendicular:** $m_2 \cdot 3 = -1 \Rightarrow m_2 = -1/3$.
</details>

### Problem 5 — Angle between lines

Find the angle between $y = 2x + 1$ and $y = -3x + 4$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\tan\theta = \left|\dfrac{m_1 - m_2}{1 + m_1 m_2}\right|$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\tan\theta = \left|\dfrac{2 - (-3)}{1 + 2 \cdot (-3)}\right| = \left|\dfrac{5}{-5}\right| = 1$,
so $\theta = \pi/4 = 45°$.
</details>

### Problem 6 — Distance from a point to a line

Find the distance from $(2, 3)$ to the line $3x - 4y + 7 = 0$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$d = \dfrac{|ax_0 + by_0 + c|}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$d = \dfrac{|3 \cdot 2 - 4 \cdot 3 + 7|}{\sqrt{9 + 16}} = \dfrac{|6 - 12 + 7|}{5} = \dfrac{1}{5}$.
</details>

### Problem 7 — Different forms

Convert $4x - 3y + 12 = 0$ to (a) slope-intercept form, (b) intercept
form $x/a + y/b = 1$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**(a)** $3y = 4x + 12 \Rightarrow y = \dfrac{4}{3} x + 4$. Slope $4/3$,
$y$-intercept $4$.

**(b)** $4x - 3y = -12 \Rightarrow \dfrac{4x}{-12} - \dfrac{3y}{-12} = 1
\Rightarrow \dfrac{x}{-3} + \dfrac{y}{4} = 1$. So $a = -3, b = 4$.
</details>

### Problem 8 — Slope from an inclination

Find the slope of a line that makes an angle of $30°$ with the *positive
$y$-axis*, measured anticlockwise.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Slope is $\tan\theta$ where $\theta$ is the angle with the positive
$x$-axis, not the $y$-axis.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Convert the angle: from the positive $y$-axis to the positive $x$-axis
adds another $90°$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Inclination with the $x$-axis: $\theta = 90° + 30° = 120°$. Then
$$ m = \tan 120° = \tan(180° - 60°) = -\tan 60° = -\sqrt{3}. $$
Negative slope — the line falls to the right, as expected of a line
tilted past the vertical.
</details>

### Problem 9 — Collinearity by slopes

Show that $A(1, 2)$, $B(3, 6)$, $C(5, 10)$ are collinear *without*
using the area formula.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Three points are collinear iff $\text{slope}\,AB = \text{slope}\,BC$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\text{slope}\,AB = (6 - 2)/(3 - 1) = 2$.
$\text{slope}\,BC = (10 - 6)/(5 - 3) = 2$.
Equal — so $A$, $B$, $C$ are collinear. (The "$\text{area} = 0$" test
from Class 10 says the same thing in different language.)
</details>

You have just used [slope](/glossary.qmd#slope), **point-slope**,
**two-point**, **slope-intercept**, **intercept**, the **angle formula**,
and the [point-line distance formula](/glossary.qmd#distance-formula).
Now we name them.

::: {.fun-fact}
**Descartes' *La Géométrie*** (1637), an appendix to his
*Discourse on the Method*, is the founding text of coordinate
geometry — but it does not contain a single coordinate axis. Descartes
preferred *one* axis (the baseline) and let the second direction be
implicit. The clean two-axis $(x, y)$ frame and the equation
$ax + by + c = 0$ for a line as we write it today were standardised
later, by mathematicians like **Lacroix** and **Monge** in the 1790s.
The intervening century-and-a-half worked the way the *intercept
form* does now — by what each axis cuts off — and only slowly
converged on the form you'll spend this chapter rearranging.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
A line in the [coordinate plane](/glossary.qmd#coordinate-plane) is
pinned by *two* facts — a point and a direction (slope), or two
points, or two intercepts. Every "form" of a line in the *Name* table
is the same line written with a different pair of facts highlighted.
The equation $Ax + By + C = 0$ is the universal form: $A$ and $B$
encode direction (slope $= -A/B$), and $C$ slides the line away from
the origin. Once you see this, switching forms becomes algebra you
already know.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Slope** of the line through $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$:
$$ m = \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} \quad (x_1 \ne x_2). $$

**Forms of the equation of a line:**

| Form | Equation |
|:--|:--|
| Slope-intercept | $y = mx + c$ |
| Point-slope | $y - y_1 = m(x - x_1)$ |
| Two-point | $\dfrac{y - y_1}{y_2 - y_1} = \dfrac{x - x_1}{x_2 - x_1}$ |
| Intercept | $\dfrac{x}{a} + \dfrac{y}{b} = 1$ |
| Normal | $x \cos\alpha + y \sin\alpha = p$ |
| General | $Ax + By + C = 0$ |

**Parallel:** $m_1 = m_2$. **Perpendicular:** $m_1 m_2 = -1$.

**Angle between two lines:**
$$ \tan\theta = \left|\dfrac{m_1 - m_2}{1 + m_1 m_2}\right|. $$

**Distance from $(x_0, y_0)$ to $Ax + By + C = 0$:**
$$ d = \dfrac{|A x_0 + B y_0 + C|}{\sqrt{A^2 + B^2}}. $$

**Distance between two parallel lines** $Ax + By + C_1 = 0$ and
$Ax + By + C_2 = 0$:
$$ d = \dfrac{|C_1 - C_2|}{\sqrt{A^2 + B^2}}. $$

**Special lines** (don't force point-slope onto these — write them
directly):

| Line | Equation |
|:--|:--|
| $x$-axis | $y = 0$ |
| $y$-axis | $x = 0$ |
| Horizontal through $(0, k)$ | $y = k$ |
| Vertical through $(h, 0)$ | $x = h$ |
| Through origin with slope $m$ | $y = mx$ |

**From general form** $Ax + By + C = 0$ with $B \ne 0$: slope $= -A/B$,
$y$-intercept $= -C/B$, $x$-intercept (if $A \ne 0$) $= -C/A$.

:::

A short verse:

> *Slope is rise upon run;*
> *Add intercept, the line is done.*
> *Multiply slopes — minus one is square;*
> *And distance steps perpendicular there.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Find the equation of the line through $(2, 3)$ that is perpendicular
> to $4x - 5y + 7 = 0$.

How a competent solver thinks:

1. Slope of the given line: $m_1 = 4/5$.
2. Perpendicular slope: $m_2 = -5/4$.
3. Through $(2, 3)$: $y - 3 = -\dfrac{5}{4}(x - 2)$.
4. Multiply by $4$: $4y - 12 = -5x + 10$, i.e. $5x + 4y - 22 = 0$.

**Answer:** $5x + 4y - 22 = 0$.

### Worked example — form conversion

> Take the line $2x + 3y - 12 = 0$. Write it in (a) slope-intercept
> form and (b) intercept form, and read off its slope, $y$-intercept,
> and $x$-intercept.

How a competent solver thinks:

1. **General $\to$ slope-intercept.** Solve for $y$:
   $3y = -2x + 12 \Rightarrow y = -\dfrac{2}{3}x + 4$.
   Slope $m = -2/3$, $y$-intercept $c = 4$.
2. **General $\to$ intercept.** Move the constant to the right and
   divide:
   $2x + 3y = 12 \Rightarrow \dfrac{x}{6} + \dfrac{y}{4} = 1$.
   So $a = 6$ (the $x$-intercept), $b = 4$ (the $y$-intercept).
3. **Sanity check.** From the general form directly: slope $= -A/B
   = -2/3$ ✓, $y$-intercept $= -C/B = 4$ ✓, $x$-intercept $= -C/A
   = 6$ ✓.

The same line; three faces. Pick whichever makes the next step
shortest.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Find the slope of the line $2x + 5y - 10 = 0$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$5y = -2x + 10 \Rightarrow y = -\dfrac{2}{5}x + 2$. Slope $= -2/5$.
</details>

**W2.** Equation of the line with $y$-intercept $4$ and slope $3$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$y = 3x + 4$.
</details>

**W3.** Distance from $(0, 0)$ to the line $3x + 4y - 25 = 0$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$d = |{-25}|/5 = 5$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Find the equation of the line through $(-1, 4)$ parallel to
$y = 3x + 7$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Same slope, different intercept.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$y - 4 = 3(x + 1) \Rightarrow y = 3x + 7$. Wait — this passes through
$(-1, 4)$? Check: $3(-1) + 7 = 4$. ✓ Same line as the given.

So actually the line is the same — the "parallel through $(-1, 4)$"
*is* $y = 3x + 7$ itself.
</details>

**P2.** Find the foot of the perpendicular from $(2, 3)$ to the line
$y = x + 1$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Perpendicular line through $(2, 3)$ with slope $-1$. Intersect with
$y = x + 1$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Perpendicular: $y - 3 = -1(x - 2) \Rightarrow y = -x + 5$.

Intersect $y = x + 1$ with $y = -x + 5$: $x + 1 = -x + 5 \Rightarrow x = 2$,
$y = 3$. Wait — that's the original point.

Recompute: $x + 1 = -x + 5 \Rightarrow 2x = 4 \Rightarrow x = 2, y = 3$.
But $(2, 3)$ is the *given* point. So the given point lies on the line?
Check: $y = x + 1$ at $x = 2$ gives $y = 3$. Yes — $(2, 3)$ *is* on the
line, foot of perpendicular is itself.

For a non-trivial example, use $(2, 5)$: perpendicular $y - 5 = -(x - 2)
\Rightarrow y = -x + 7$. Intersect with $y = x + 1$: $x + 1 = -x + 7
\Rightarrow x = 3, y = 4$. **Foot at $(3, 4)$.**
</details>

**P3.** Find the equation of the line passing through $(3, -2)$ that is
parallel to the line joining $(2, 1)$ and $(5, -3)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Slope of joining line: $(-3 - 1)/(5 - 2) = -4/3$.

Through $(3, -2)$ with slope $-4/3$:
$y + 2 = -\dfrac{4}{3}(x - 3) \Rightarrow 3y + 6 = -4x + 12 \Rightarrow 4x + 3y - 6 = 0$.
</details>

**P4 — linear model.** When milk costs $\textsf{Rs}\,14$ per litre, the
daily demand is $980$ litres; at $\textsf{Rs}\,16$ per litre, demand
falls to $1220$ litres. Wait — *falls?* Real demand curves slope down,
but the data says otherwise; treat the numbers as given and assume
demand $D$ is a linear function of price $P$. Find $D(P)$ and estimate
the demand at $\textsf{Rs}\,17$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
You have two data points $(P, D) = (14, 980)$ and $(16, 1220)$. Use
the two-point form.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Compute the slope first; then plug into point-slope.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Slope $= (1220 - 980)/(16 - 14) = 240/2 = 120$.
Point-slope through $(14, 980)$:
$D - 980 = 120(P - 14) \Rightarrow D = 120P - 700$.
At $P = 17$: $D = 120 \cdot 17 - 700 = 1340$ litres.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Find the equation of the line that passes through $(2, 3)$ and
makes an angle of $45°$ with the line $x + y = 1$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Slope of given: $-1$. Use $\tan 45° = |(m + 1)/(1 - m)| = 1$. Two
solutions.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\left|\dfrac{m - (-1)}{1 + m(-1)}\right| = 1 \Rightarrow |m + 1| = |1 - m|$.

Case 1: $m + 1 = 1 - m \Rightarrow m = 0$. Line $y = 3$.

Case 2: $m + 1 = -(1 - m) \Rightarrow m + 1 = m - 1$, no solution
(meaning the perpendicular).

Wait, the second case makes the line parallel to the y-axis: $x = 2$.

**Answers:** $y = 3$ and $x = 2$.
</details>

**S2.** Find the area of the triangle formed by the lines $x = 0$,
$y = 0$, and $3x + 4y = 12$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Find vertices, use $\dfrac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$3x + 4y = 12$ meets $y = 0$ at $(4, 0)$, meets $x = 0$ at $(0, 3)$.
Origin is the third vertex.

Area $= \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot 4 \cdot 3 = 6$.
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**1-mark (objective).** Slope of the line $\sqrt{3} x - y + 5 = 0$ is
(a) $\sqrt{3}$ (b) $-\sqrt{3}$ (c) $1/\sqrt{3}$ (d) $-1/\sqrt{3}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$y = \sqrt{3}x + 5$. Slope $= \sqrt{3}$. **Answer: (a).**
</details>

**2-mark (short answer).** Find the angle between $y = x$ and $y = 0$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$m_1 = 1, m_2 = 0$. $\tan\theta = |1 - 0|/|1 + 0| = 1$. So $\theta = 45°$.
</details>

**3-mark (short answer II).** Find the equation of the line through
$(1, 2)$ and parallel to the line $2x - 3y + 7 = 0$. Then find the
distance between these two lines.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Same slope $2/3$. After finding the new line, use the parallel-line
distance formula.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
New line: $y - 2 = \dfrac{2}{3}(x - 1) \Rightarrow 2x - 3y + 4 = 0$.

Distance: $\dfrac{|7 - 4|}{\sqrt{4 + 9}} = \dfrac{3}{\sqrt{13}}$.
</details>

**5-mark (long answer).** A triangle has vertices $A(2, 3)$, $B(6, 5)$,
$C(0, 7)$. Find:

(i) The equation of side $BC$.
(ii) The equation of the median from $A$.
(iii) The equation of the altitude from $A$.
(iv) The length of the altitude from $A$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Median goes to the midpoint of $BC$. Altitude is perpendicular to $BC$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**(i)** Slope $BC = (7 - 5)/(0 - 6) = -1/3$. Line through $B(6, 5)$:
$y - 5 = -\dfrac{1}{3}(x - 6) \Rightarrow x + 3y - 21 = 0$.

**(ii)** Midpoint $M$ of $BC = ((6+0)/2, (5+7)/2) = (3, 6)$.
Slope $AM = (6 - 3)/(3 - 2) = 3$. Through $A(2, 3)$:
$y - 3 = 3(x - 2) \Rightarrow 3x - y - 3 = 0$.

**(iii)** Altitude perpendicular to $BC$. Slope = $3$ (negative reciprocal
of $-1/3$). Through $A(2, 3)$: same as the median equation $3x - y - 3 = 0$
— *coincidence*: in this triangle the median and altitude from $A$ may
coincide (this happens for isoceles triangles with $AB = AC$).

Check: $|AB| = \sqrt{16 + 4} = \sqrt{20}$. $|AC| = \sqrt{4 + 16} = \sqrt{20}$.
Yes — isosceles. Median = altitude.

**(iv)** Length: distance from $A(2, 3)$ to line $x + 3y - 21 = 0$:
$\dfrac{|2 + 9 - 21|}{\sqrt{1 + 9}} = \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{10}} = \sqrt{10}$.
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Exemplar Ch 9 LA on triangles with given
vertices matches the 5-mark item.
**JEE pointer:** the angle-between-lines and foot-of-perpendicular
formulas (Stretch S1, P2) are JEE Main standard.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **A line is two facts.** Point-and-slope, two points, two intercepts — pick the pair the question gives you and use the matching form. The six forms in the *Name* table are translations of one another, not separate ideas.
2. **Parallel means $m_1 = m_2$, perpendicular means $m_1 m_2 = -1$.** That product-equals-$-1$ is the cleanest fact in plane geometry — and it converts "find the perpendicular line" into a one-line slope swap.
3. **Distance from $(x_0, y_0)$ to $Ax + By + C = 0$ is $|Ax_0 + By_0 + C|/\sqrt{A^2 + B^2}$.** Memorise it; it answers "how close is the player to the wall?" and "what is the height of the triangle from this vertex?" with no extra machinery.

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Vertical lines have slope $0$."** They have **undefined** slope
   (it's $\tan 90°$, which blows up). Horizontal lines have slope $0$.
   The two cases are not interchangeable.
2. **"Perpendicular means $m_1 + m_2 = 0$."** It means $m_1 m_2 = -1$.
   Sum-to-zero would be $m_2 = -m_1$ — that's the *reflection* across
   the $y$-axis, not perpendicularity.
3. **"Distance from a point to a line is the distance to the closest
   $x$-intercept."** No — the perpendicular distance is the unique
   shortest path. The formula handles all cases.
4. **Slope sign-flip from inconsistent labelling.** Always set up
   $(y_2 - y_1)/(x_2 - x_1)$ with the *same* point as "$2$" in both
   numerator and denominator. Mixing them ($y_2 - y_1$ on top but
   $x_1 - x_2$ on the bottom) silently flips the sign of $m$.
5. **Intercept form for a line through the origin.** If both intercepts
   are zero, $x/a + y/b = 1$ is meaningless — there is no $a$, no $b$.
   Use $y = mx$ (or $Ax + By = 0$) instead.
6. **"$2$ units to the left of the origin" $\to x = 2$.** No — left
   means negative, so $x = -2$. Drawing a quick number line before
   substituting prevents this.
:::

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you write down 4 different forms of the equation of a line on
   demand?
2. Did you solve at least 5 of the 7 *Discover* problems and 5 of the 7
   practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?
3. Can you handle a triangle problem (vertices, sides, medians,
   altitudes) end-to-end?

- **3 yes:** move on to **[Conic Sections (Circle and Parabola)](../10a-conic-sections-circle-parabola/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo Stretch S2 and the 5-mark item.
- **0–1 yes:** drill the line-equation forms by re-deriving each from
  the slope-intercept.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[Class 10 — Coordinate Geometry](../../tier-cbse-10-class-10/07-coordinate-geometry/index.qmd)** —
  distance, midpoint, section formulas.
- **[Class 9 — Linear Equations in Two Variables](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/04-linear-equations-two-vars/index.qmd)** —
  the equation $ax + by = c$ as a line.

### What this unlocks

- **[Conic Sections (Circle and Parabola)](../10a-conic-sections-circle-parabola/index.qmd)** —
  tangents and chords are special straight lines.
- **[Class 12 — Linear Programming](../../tier-cbse-12-class-12/12-linear-programming/index.qmd)** —
  feasible region boundaries.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 11, Chapter 9 — Straight Lines** (NCERT 2023 rationalised).
  Sections 9.1–9.6: slope, various forms, distance from a point,
  distance between parallel lines.
- **Sister synthesis lessons:**
  [`t2.coordinate-plane`](../../tier-2-prealgebra/coordinate-plane/index.qmd) and
  [`t2.linear-functions-graphs`](../../tier-2-prealgebra/linear-functions-graphs/index.qmd).

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Collision detection: "is the player on the wall?"
becomes "is the point above or below the wall's line?" — a sign-of-$Ax+By+C$
check, computed thousands of times per frame.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** A linear classifier (perceptron, logistic regression with
linear features) draws a line $w_1 x + w_2 y + b = 0$ between two
classes. Learning the weights is learning the line.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

Touchstones: 3Blue1Brown (slope as direction vector), Russian school
(Discover sequence forces all six forms to crystallise), Bhāskara
(verse on slope-product perpendicularity).
:::
